description_long: This is a World War II-era four-engine bomber airplane, resembling the Boeing B-17 Flying Fortress, with a robust metallic fuselage and a classic tail fin marked with a red tip and a yellow 'W' insignia. The wings are straight and mid-mounted, each equipped with two propeller-driven engines, and the wingtips are painted red for visibility. The fuselage is primarily silver-gray with U.S. Air Force roundels and black identification markings along the sides. The cockpit is located at the forward end of the fuselage, featuring a glazed nose section typical of bomber aircraft from this era. The tail section includes a single vertical stabilizer and horizontal stabilizers, both with red accents. The overall design is utilitarian, with visible gun turrets and hatches, indicating its military function.
description: A silver-gray, four-engine propeller bomber with red-tipped wings and tail, military markings, and a forward glass cockpit. The design features a robust fuselage, mid-mounted straight wings, and a single vertical tail fin.
description_view_0: Side view showing the full length of the fuselage, four engines, red-tipped wings and tail, and military insignia.
description_view_1: Top-down view highlighting the straight mid-mounted wings, red wingtips, and the symmetrical engine arrangement.
description_view_2: Opposite side view displaying the tail markings, engine nacelles, and the overall streamlined profile.
description_view_3: Frontal view emphasizing the four propeller engines, cockpit glazing, and the broad wingspan.
attribute_wingspan_configuration: Mid-wing, straight wings with no visible winglets, slight dihedral angle, four engine nacelles mounted on the wings.
attribute_engine_type: Four piston engines, two per wing, each with a visible propeller.
attribute_tail_design: Conventional tail with a single vertical stabilizer and two horizontal stabilizers.
attribute_landing_gear_type: Retractable, tricycle landing gear with main gear under the wings and a nose gear.
attribute_cockpit_placement: Forward-mounted, enclosed cockpit with multi-seat configuration and a glazed nose section.
